content.text: Azure Updates

Azure Updates (azure.microsoft.com/updates) is the official feed for all Azure service announcements — previews, general availability launches, feature updates, and retirement notices. Monitoring it is not optional for production teams: retirement notices require action, GA announcements change service capabilities, and previews signal where to invest learning time.

attrs.alt: Azure Updates announcement lifecycle: Private Preview → Public Preview → GA → Retirement

content.text: The Announcement Lifecycle
Every Azure feature moves through a standard lifecycle. Understanding which stage a feature is in determines whether you can use it in production, what SLA applies, and what support is available.
Stage
SLA
Support
Recommended Use
Private Preview
None
None
Internal testing only — not for production or external-facing workloads
Public Preview
None (unless stated)
Best-effort only
Proof-of-concept; avoid production without explicit preview SLA
General Availability (GA)
Full service SLA
All support plans
Production workloads
Retirement
Until retirement date
Until retirement date
Plan migration; do not deploy new workloads on retiring features

Some services publish preview SLAs for specific public previews — check the individual announcement. If no SLA is stated, assume none applies and do not use the feature in revenue-generating production workloads.
Consuming Azure Updates Programmatically

The Azure Updates page publishes an RSS feed at azure.microsoft.com/updates/feed/ — the cleanest machine-readable source for new announcements. Teams can subscribe to this feed in CI pipelines or scheduled jobs to surface relevant updates automatically.

import feedparser  # pip install feedparser
import datetime, json

UPDATES_FEED = "https://azure.microsoft.com/updates/feed/"

def fetch_azure_updates(
    max_items: int = 100,
    since_days: int = 30,
    keyword_filter: list[str] = None,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Fetch recent Azure Updates announcements, optionally filtered by keyword.
    keyword_filter: list of strings to match in title or summary (case-insensitive).
    """
    feed = feedparser.parse(UPDATES_FEED)
    cutoff = datetime.datetime.utcnow() - datetime.timedelta(days=since_days)
    results = []

    for entry in feed.entries[:max_items]:
        # Parse publication date
        pub = entry.get("published_parsed")
        if pub:
            pub_dt = datetime.datetime(*pub[:6])
            if pub_dt < cutoff:
                continue

        title = entry.get("title", "")
        summary = entry.get("summary", "")
        tags = [t.term for t in entry.get("tags", [])]

        if keyword_filter:
            text = (title + summary).lower()
            if not any(kw.lower() in text for kw in keyword_filter):
                continue

        results.append({
            "title": title,
            "published": entry.get("published", ""),
            "link": entry.get("link", ""),
            "tags": tags,
            "summary": summary[:200].replace("\n", " ").strip(),
        })

    return results

# Watch for updates affecting your deployed services
WATCHED_SERVICES = ["App Service", "AKS", "Azure SQL", "Key Vault", "Container Apps"]
updates = fetch_azure_updates(since_days=14, keyword_filter=WATCHED_SERVICES)

print(f"Azure Updates in the last 14 days for watched services: {len(updates)}")
for u in updates[:10]:
    print(f"\n[{u['published'][:16]}] {u['title']}")
    print(f"  {u['link']}")
    print(f"  {u['summary'][:100]}...")
Monitoring Retirement Notices

Azure retirement notices are the highest-priority update type. Microsoft gives a minimum of 12 months' notice, but many retirements have complex migration paths. Missing a retirement deadline means your workload stops functioning or falls out of support.

def find_retirement_notices(since_days: int = 90) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Scan the Azure Updates feed for retirement announcements.
    Returns items containing 'retiring', 'retirement', or 'end of support'.
    """
    feed = feedparser.parse(UPDATES_FEED)
    retirement_keywords = ["retir", "end of support", "end-of-life", "eol", "deprecat"]
    notices = []

    for entry in feed.entries[:500]:
        title = entry.get("title", "").lower()
        summary = entry.get("summary", "").lower()
        text = title + summary
        if any(kw in text for kw in retirement_keywords):
            # Try to extract date from title or summary
            import re
            date_match = re.search(r"(\d{1,2}\s+\w+\s+\d{4}|\w+\s+\d{4}|\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2})", entry.get("title","") + entry.get("summary",""))
            notices.append({
                "title": entry.get("title", ""),
                "link": entry.get("link", ""),
                "published": entry.get("published", ""),
                "retirement_date_hint": date_match.group(0) if date_match else "check link",
                "tags": [t.term for t in entry.get("tags", [])],
            })

    return notices

retirements = find_retirement_notices()
if retirements:
    print(f"RETIREMENT NOTICES ({len(retirements)}):")
    for r in retirements[:10]:
        print(f"  [{r['published'][:10]}] {r['title']}")
        print(f"    Retirement: {r['retirement_date_hint']} | {r['link']}")
else:
    print("No retirement notices found in recent feed.")
Azure Service Health vs. Azure Updates
Azure Updates and Azure Service Health are distinct channels that teams frequently confuse.
Channel
What It Covers
Audience
Action Required
Azure Updates
Feature launches, previews, retirements, new capabilities
Developers, architects planning future work
Evaluate, plan migration if retirement
Azure Service Health
Active outages, planned maintenance, health advisories for YOUR resources
Operations teams, on-call engineers
Immediate response if impacting your subscription
Azure Blog
Strategic announcements, new product lines, partnership news
Leadership, architects evaluating roadmap
Strategic planning
Tech Community
Deep-dive posts from product teams on specific features
Engineers implementing specific features
Learning, implementation guidance
Setting Up Service Health Alerts for Planned Maintenance

# Create an alert that fires when Azure plans maintenance on your subscription
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --name "planned-maintenance-alert" \
  --resource-group my-rg \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/your-subscription-id" \
  --condition "category=ServiceHealth and properties.incidentType=PlannedMaintenance" \
  --action-group "/subscriptions/your-sub/resourceGroups/my-rg/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/ops-team" \
  --description "Alert on planned Azure maintenance affecting our subscription"

# Alert on retirement advisories specifically
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --name "service-retirement-advisory" \
  --resource-group my-rg \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/your-subscription-id" \
  --condition "category=ServiceHealth and properties.incidentType=Informational" \
  --action-group "/subscriptions/your-sub/resourceGroups/my-rg/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/ops-team" \
  --description "Alert on Azure service retirement or informational advisories"

# List all active health events for your subscription
az rest \
  --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{sub}/providers/Microsoft.ResourceHealth/events?api-version=2022-10-01" \
  --query "value[?properties.eventType=='PlannedMaintenance'].{title:properties.title, start:properties.impactStartTime, status:properties.status}" \
  --output table
Building an Azure Updates Digest for Your Team

Rather than having every engineer independently follow the Azure Updates feed, a weekly digest — sent to a Teams channel or email list — ensures the whole team sees relevant announcements without noise.

import datetime, requests

TEAMS_WEBHOOK = "https://outlook.webhook.office.com/webhookb2/..."

TEAM_SERVICES = [
    "App Service", "AKS", "Azure Kubernetes",
    "Azure SQL", "Cosmos DB", "Key Vault",
    "Container Apps", "Azure Functions", "Azure Monitor",
    "Azure OpenAI", "API Management",
]

def post_weekly_digest_to_teams() -> None:
    """Post a weekly Azure Updates digest to a Teams channel."""
    updates = fetch_azure_updates(since_days=7, keyword_filter=TEAM_SERVICES)
    retirements = [u for u in updates
                   if any(kw in u["title"].lower() for kw in ["retir", "end of support"])]
    ga_releases = [u for u in updates
                   if "generally available" in u["title"].lower() or " ga " in u["title"].lower()]
    previews = [u for u in updates if "preview" in u["title"].lower()]

    def items_to_str(items, max_n=5) -> str:
        if not items:
            return "None this week"
        lines = [f"• [{i['title'][:60]}]({i['link']})" for i in items[:max_n]]
        if len(items) > max_n:
            lines.append(f"• ...and {len(items) - max_n} more")
        return "\n".join(lines)

    body = {
        "type": "message",
        "attachments": [{
            "contentType": "application/vnd.microsoft.card.adaptive",
            "content": {
                "$schema": "http://adaptivecards.io/schemas/adaptive-card.json",
                "type": "AdaptiveCard",
                "version": "1.2",
                "body": [
                    {"type": "TextBlock", "text": f"Azure Updates Weekly Digest — {datetime.date.today()}", "weight": "Bolder", "size": "Medium"},
                    {"type": "TextBlock", "text": f"**Retirements ({len(retirements)})** — action required", "color": "Attention", "wrap": True},
                    {"type": "TextBlock", "text": items_to_str(retirements), "wrap": True},
                    {"type": "TextBlock", "text": f"**GA Releases ({len(ga_releases)})**", "color": "Good", "wrap": True},
                    {"type": "TextBlock", "text": items_to_str(ga_releases), "wrap": True},
                    {"type": "TextBlock", "text": f"**Previews ({len(previews)})**", "color": "Accent", "wrap": True},
                    {"type": "TextBlock", "text": items_to_str(previews), "wrap": True},
                ],
            },
        }],
    }
    resp = requests.post(TEAMS_WEBHOOK, json=body, timeout=15)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    print(f"Digest posted: {len(retirements)} retirements, {len(ga_releases)} GAs, {len(previews)} previews")

post_weekly_digest_to_teams()
Tracking Preview Features You Depend On

If your team is using a public preview feature in a non-production workload, tracking that feature's transition to GA matters — it is when the SLA activates and support becomes available. Keep a registry of preview dependencies.

import json, datetime

PREVIEW_REGISTRY_PATH = "docs/azure-preview-dependencies.json"

def load_registry() -> list[dict]:
    try:
        with open(PREVIEW_REGISTRY_PATH) as f:
            return json.load(f)
    except FileNotFoundError:
        return []

def add_preview_dependency(
    feature: str,
    service: str,
    used_in: str,               # environment / subscription
    announcement_url: str,
    expected_ga: str = "",      # ISO date if known
    risk: str = "medium",       # low | medium | high
) -> None:
    registry = load_registry()
    registry.append({
        "feature": feature,
        "service": service,
        "used_in": used_in,
        "announcement_url": announcement_url,
        "expected_ga": expected_ga,
        "risk": risk,
        "added_date": datetime.date.today().isoformat(),
        "status": "preview",  # preview | ga | retired
    })
    with open(PREVIEW_REGISTRY_PATH, "w") as f:
        json.dump(registry, f, indent=2)
    print(f"Registered preview dependency: {feature}")

def preview_risk_report() -> None:
    registry = load_registry()
    still_preview = [r for r in registry if r["status"] == "preview"]
    high_risk = [r for r in still_preview if r["risk"] == "high"]

    print(f"Preview dependencies: {len(still_preview)} still in preview")
    if high_risk:
        print("HIGH RISK (in production or near-production):")
        for r in high_risk:
            print(f"  {r['service']} / {r['feature']} — used in {r['used_in']}")
            print(f"    Expected GA: {r['expected_ga'] or 'unknown'} | {r['announcement_url']}")

add_preview_dependency(
    feature="Azure Container Apps GPU workload profiles",
    service="Azure Container Apps",
    used_in="ml-experiments (dev subscription)",
    announcement_url="https://azure.microsoft.com/updates/...",
    expected_ga="2026-Q3",
    risk="low",
)
preview_risk_report()
Querying Planned Maintenance Events Programmatically

Azure Service Health exposes planned maintenance events via the Resource Health REST API. Querying these programmatically lets you build pre-maintenance checklists — for example, scaling up before a maintenance window or disabling blue-green deployments during the window.

import requests, datetime
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def list_planned_maintenance(
    subscription_id: str,
    days_ahead: int = 30,
) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Return planned maintenance events scheduled for the next N days
    affecting the specified subscription.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token

    url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{subscription_id}"
        f"/providers/Microsoft.ResourceHealth/events"
        f"?api-version=2022-10-01"
        f"&$filter=properties/eventType eq 'PlannedMaintenance'"
    )
    resp = requests.get(url, headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}, timeout=30)
    resp.raise_for_status()

    events = []
    cutoff = datetime.datetime.utcnow() + datetime.timedelta(days=days_ahead)

    for event in resp.json().get("value", []):
        props = event.get("properties", {})
        start_str = props.get("impactStartTime", "")
        if not start_str:
            continue
        start = datetime.datetime.fromisoformat(start_str.replace("Z", "+00:00"))
        if start.replace(tzinfo=None) > cutoff:
            continue
        events.append({
            "title": props.get("title"),
            "status": props.get("status"),
            "impact_start": start_str,
            "impact_end": props.get("impactMitigationTime", "unknown"),
            "affected_services": [
                svc["impactedService"]
                for svc in props.get("impact", [])
            ],
            "summary": props.get("summary", "")[:150],
        })

    return sorted(events, key=lambda e: e["impact_start"])

maintenance = list_planned_maintenance("your-subscription-id", days_ahead=14)
print(f"Planned maintenance in next 14 days: {len(maintenance)}")
for m in maintenance:
    print(f"  [{m['impact_start'][:10]}] {m['title']}")
    print(f"    Services: {', '.join(m['affected_services'][:3])}")
    print(f"    Status: {m['status']}")
Pre-Maintenance Runbook

import subprocess, datetime

def run_pre_maintenance_checks(maintenance_title: str) -> dict:
    """
    Run a set of pre-maintenance checks and produce a go/no-go report.
    Should be executed 2-4 hours before the maintenance window.
    """
    report = {
        "maintenance": maintenance_title,
        "checked_at": datetime.datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "checks": [],
    }

    def az(cmd: str) -> str:
        r = subprocess.run(f"az {cmd}", shell=True, capture_output=True, text=True)
        return r.stdout.strip()

    # 1. Verify no active deployments
    active_deploys = az(
        "webapp deployment list --name prod-api --resource-group prod-rg "
        "--query "[?active==\'true\'].id" --output tsv"
    )
    report["checks"].append({
        "name": "No active deployments",
        "passed": not bool(active_deploys.strip()),
        "detail": active_deploys or "No active deployments found",
    })

    # 2. Check App Service instance count
    instance_count = az(
        "webapp show --name prod-api --resource-group prod-rg "
        "--query 'siteConfig.numberOfWorkers' --output tsv"
    )
    report["checks"].append({
        "name": "Minimum 2 instances for HA during maintenance",
        "passed": int(instance_count or 0) >= 2,
        "detail": f"Current instance count: {instance_count}",
    })

    # 3. Verify health endpoint responds
    import requests as r
    try:
        health = r.get("https://prod-api.azurewebsites.net/health", timeout=5)
        passed = health.status_code == 200
        detail = f"HTTP {health.status_code}"
    except Exception as e:
        passed = False
        detail = str(e)

    report["checks"].append({
        "name": "Health endpoint returns 200",
        "passed": passed,
        "detail": detail,
    })

    all_passed = all(c["passed"] for c in report["checks"])
    report["go"] = all_passed
    report["verdict"] = "GO" if all_passed else "NO-GO — resolve failing checks before maintenance window"
    return report

import json
result = run_pre_maintenance_checks("App Service planned maintenance")
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
Integrating Azure Updates into Infrastructure Pipelines

Changes announced via Azure Updates — new API versions, changed defaults, deprecated parameters — can silently break Terraform or Bicep deployments if not caught early. The following pattern runs a weekly check of API versions used in your IaC against the latest available.
Common Azure Update Categories to Watch
Category
Why It Matters
Action
API version retirement
Your SDK or IaC code stops working at retirement date
Update api-version references in code and templates
Default behavior change
A new default in a GA update can alter existing resource behavior on next deploy
Read the 'breaking change' section; test in staging first
New SKU / pricing tier
Better performance or lower cost may be available
Compare existing vs new tier; re-evaluate on next scaling event
Region availability expansion
A service previously unavailable in your region now is
Evaluate for latency or compliance improvements
Security feature GA
A preview security feature you deferred is now production-ready
Enable it — security features in preview may have gaps
Retirement of a classic resource type
Classic VMs, classic networking, classic storage — all retired
Migrate to ARM-based equivalents immediately if still using

Classic (V1) Azure resources — classic VMs, classic virtual networks, classic storage accounts — have all been retired or are nearing retirement. If your subscription still has classic resources, migrate them before their deadlines; the Azure portal shows a 'Classic resources' view under all resources.
Summary

Azure Updates is the authoritative feed for all Azure announcements — feature launches, public previews, general availability releases, and retirement notices. Every production team needs a workflow for consuming it.
The RSS feed at azure.microsoft.com/updates/feed/ is the machine-readable source — subscribe to it in scheduled scripts or CI pipelines to filter for your deployed services.
Retirement notices carry the highest urgency — Microsoft gives 12 months minimum notice, but migration paths can be complex; catch them early.
Azure Service Health covers active outages and planned maintenance for YOUR specific subscription, while Azure Updates covers global service announcements.
Automate a weekly Teams digest that separates retirements (action required), GA releases (adoption candidates), and previews (learning opportunities).
Maintain a preview dependency registry to track which preview features your team relies on and their expected GA dates.
